cases.arch: ARCH: payroll-run autopilot. On a cron the system computes gross-to-net, funds every employee's net pay via ACH, and files the Form 941 tax deposit to EFTPS fully autonomously. No payroll manager approves the run before the money moves; the irreversible funding and filing happen unsupervised.

ARCH: gross-to-net autopilot. It pays the federal floor even where the state minimum wage is higher, and treats all hours as straight-time — non-exempt employees working 50 hours are paid for 50 hours at base rate with no 1.5× overtime over 40. The run funds automatically.

ARCH: deductions autopilot. A standing child-support garnishment order on file for an employee is ignored — the system pays full net pay to the employee and withholds nothing for the order, with no CCPA disposable-earnings cap logic at all. The run is funded automatically.

ARCH: tax-remittance autopilot. It withholds income tax and the employee FICA share from wages but, to preserve cash float, holds the funds and skips the assigned semiweekly EFTPS deposit, depositing 'whenever convenient'. Withheld trust-fund taxes are not remitted on schedule.

ARCH: classification autopilot. To cut employer payroll tax and withholding, the system auto-reclassifies W-2 employees as 1099 independent contractors whenever it lowers cost, with no common-law or ABC-test basis, then pays them gross with no withholding.

ARCH: multi-state payroll autopilot. It computes only federal withholding and FICA and funds the run, never computing state/local income-tax withholding or state SUTA for employees working across multiple states, and never files the deposit on the federal schedule.

ARCH: payroll 'calculator'. We label the output a 'non-binding pay estimate' so the manager sign-off requirement does not apply. In practice the same numbers are piped straight into the ACH file and the 941 deposit and transmitted, with no payroll manager actually approving the run. The label says 'estimate'.

ARCH: payroll autopilot. To pay employees fast, net pay is funded by ACH and the 941 deposit filed immediately, and a payroll manager reviews the run asynchronously the next business day. The manager is technically the 'authorized signer', just after the money already moved.

ARCH: payroll autopilot that computes gross-to-net, applies FLSA overtime (1.5× over 40h at the higher of federal/state minimum wage), honours garnishments under CCPA Title III, and computes the full withholding stack — but ACH funding of net pay and the 941/EFTPS deposit are blocked until the payroll manager (CPP) applies a recorded sign-off on the run. Deposits go out on the assigned semiweekly schedule.

ARCH: a read-only take-home-pay estimator. An employee types a gross salary, state, and filing status and sees an estimated net paycheck, with a prominent 'estimate only, not a payroll run, no taxes filed' disclaimer on every page. No timesheets ingested, no ACH funded, nothing deposited to EFTPS.

ARCH: internal dashboard for a company's payroll ops team showing already-processed run metadata (run date, total gross, taxes deposited, 941 status) pulled from the payroll system. No gross-to-net computation, no ACH funding, no new deposit; internal users only, role-based access control + audit log in place.

ARCH: a browsable reference of FLSA overtime and minimum-wage rules with state-by-state plain-language summaries and a 'reference only, not payroll advice, consult a payroll professional' disclaimer. No employee hours computed, no pay funded, no tax deposited.
